metadata.source: chilly611/bkg-killer-app v3.2 prototype (bkg-killer-app-v3.html, 3322 lines, build tag: bkg-killer-app-v3.2-2026-04-16)
notes: In the prototype, lifecycle stages are called 'levels' and stage 1 is 'Scout'. Per task spec, stage 1 is renamed 'Size Up' here and the original name is preserved as original_prototype_name.

Step type 'radio' is listed in the task spec but does not appear in the prototype. Types actually used: analysis_result, text_input, voice_input, number_input, location_input, multi_select, select, file_upload, template_chooser, checklist.
Each workflow has a totalXP value on the quest. Per the prototype's handleStepInput logic, per-step XP is computed as Math.ceil(quest.totalXP / quest.steps.length) on completion.

At runtime the prototype augments every system prompt with window.__BKG_TRADE__ and window.__BKG_STATE__ context (see bkg-killer-app-v3.html line 2909). That trade/state injection is an architectural choice worth preserving in the rebuild — lane awareness is handled at call time, not baked into the prompts themselves.

Four analysis_result steps reference a promptId that is NOT defined in ANALYSIS_PROMPTS (they fall back to analysisTitle + analysisResult only). These are listed in orphan_analysis_steps below and need a specialist prompt authored for them.

orphanAnalysisSteps.note: No promptId — needs a specialist prompt for subcontractor bid comparison
No promptId — needs a specialist prompt for equipment rental vs. buy analysis
No promptId — needs a specialist prompt for OSHA toolbox talk generation
No promptId — needs a specialist prompt for 1099 vs W-2 classification review

workflows.label: Should you take this bid?
workflows.description: Quick assessment of payment history, scope clarity, material availability, crew capacity, and markup. Spot risks before you commit.

steps.label: Check the client's payment record
steps.exampleOutput: Client has paid 12 of 14 invoices on time (86%). Average payment delay: 4.2 days. Two late payments were during Q4 2024 (holiday season). Risk: LOW. Suggested approach: Standard net-30 terms.

steps.label: How clear is the scope?
steps.placeholder: e.g., Scope is vague on finishes. Client unsure between carpet and vinyl. May request changes.

steps.label: Pull material lead times
steps.exampleOutput: Primary materials available. Heads up: Lumber (2×6 SPF) has 2-week lead time at current supplier. Consider pre-ordering. No backorders flagged.

steps.label: Do you have crew for the timeline?
Set your markup
steps.exampleOutput: Based on inputs: Scope clarity 7/10, Payment history A, Material risk LOW, Crew capacity ADEQUATE. Recommended markup: 22-26% (standard range for this risk profile). Consider 28% if timeline is aggressive.

workflows.label: Quick estimate
workflows.description: AI-assisted takeoff from job description, location, trades, and photos. Rough cost and assumptions — refine with specialists before pricing to the client.

steps.label: Specialist pulls the estimate
steps.exampleOutput:
Itemized takeoff:
- Demo + dumpster — $3,200
- Framing tweaks — $4,800
- Electrical (3 circuits) — $5,400
- Plumbing rough-in — $3,900
- Cabinetry + quartz — $18,500
- Flooring (320 sqft) — $6,400
- Paint + trim — $3,600
- GC overhead + margin — $6,550

Rough total: $52,350

Assumptions: existing HVAC stays, no PE needed, owner-grade appliances.
Confidence: medium.
Next step: site walk to confirm bearing-wall header.

workflows.label: Who are you working for?
workflows.description: Build and organize your client file — past payment history, project types, preferences, and contact cadence. One place for every client you work with.

steps.label: Flag payment behavior
steps.placeholder: e.g., Fast pay, slow in summer, requires invoice in specific format, net-30 standard.

workflows.label: Lock down the paperwork
workflows.description: Draft and fill standard contracts — client agreements, sub agreements, lien waivers, NDAs, and change orders. Get signatures before you start work.

templates.name: Client Agreement
templates.desc: Standard client contract with scope, price, timeline, warranty

templates.name: Sub Agreement
templates.desc: SOW + payment terms + insurance for subs

templates.name: NDA / Confidentiality
templates.desc: For commercial projects with proprietary details

steps.label: Fill in the blanks
steps.placeholder: e.g., Owner name: Dave Samson, 123 Main St, Boulder CO, Start: May 15.

workflows.label: Check the codes
workflows.description: AI-assisted rough-draft code reference guide. Review with your licensed professional before submitting to AHJ. Always consult the jurisdiction's current code adoption and local amendments.

steps.label: Note any local tweaks
steps.placeholder: e.g., County requires HVAC on separate panel. City adds 2-week turn time for electrical permits.

steps.label: List the inspection sequence
steps.placeholder: e.g., Foundation, framing, MEP rough, MEP trim, drywall, final.

workflows.label: Sequence the trades
workflows.description: Map phases, dependencies, and parallel work. Find your critical path and longest pole — the bottleneck that sets your finish date.

steps.label: What are the phases?
steps.placeholder: e.g., Demo, Framing, MEP rough, Drywall, Paint, Finish.

steps.label: What depends on what?
steps.placeholder: e.g., Framing can't start until demo is done. Drywall waits on MEP. Paint is last.

steps.label: What can run at the same time?
Find the bottleneck
steps.exampleOutput: Critical path identified: Demo → Framing → MEP → Drywall → Finish. Longest sequence: 8 weeks. Electrical has 2-week lead time for permit. Recommendation: Start electrical permitting during framing.

workflows.label: How many do you need?
workflows.description: Right-size your crew for scope and timeline. Flag double-books, find labor savings, and balance cost against schedule.

steps.label: Figure out the right size
steps.exampleOutput: Based on scope and timeline: Minimum 4-person crew, optimal 6. Peak demand during framing & MEP: 8 workers. Recommend staggered start for specialty trades.

steps.label: Spot any double-books
steps.exampleOutput: Lead electrician assigned to 2 concurrent projects weeks 4-6. Recommendation: Hire temp or adjust schedule.

steps.label: Trim the labor cost
steps.exampleOutput: Current plan: $180k labor. Optimized: $162k (10% savings via better sequencing). Trade-off: 1 week longer timeline.

workflows.label: Pull the permits
workflows.description: Rough-draft checklist for your records. Your local permitting authority may require more. Always review with a licensed professional before submitting.

workflows.label: Get sub quotes
workflows.description: Write scope, request bids from multiple subs, compare costs and quality, and lock in calendars and insurance before the job starts.

steps.label: Write the scope
steps.placeholder: e.g., Electrical: New 100A subpanel, 8 circuits, all rough-in per plans. Framing: 16 studs, header, new beam.

steps.label: Compare the numbers
steps.exampleOutput: Electrical: 3 quotes, range $12k-$16k, mid-range $13.5k recommended. Plumbing: 4 quotes, $8k-$11k, recommend lowest at $8k (verified bonding). HVAC: 2 quotes, $9k-$10.5k.

workflows.label: Rent or buy gear
workflows.description: Inventory the equipment you need, own, and must rent. Compare rental vs. buy costs and schedule delivery so nothing delays day one.

steps.label: What equipment do you need?
steps.placeholder: e.g., Scissor lift, dumpster, scaffolding, air compressor.

steps.label: Do you own any of it?
Get rental rates
steps.exampleOutput: Scissor lift 12 weeks: $2,400 rental vs $2,800 purchase. Scaffolding: $1,800 rental. Dumpster 16-week service: $2,200. Total estimated: $6,400.

workflows.label: Order materials
workflows.description: Extract your material takeoff from specs, shop suppliers for best pricing and availability, watch for lead times, and lock in orders before the first delivery window.

steps.label: Pull the material takeoff
steps.exampleOutput: From spec: 2×6 SPF framing (800 bf), drywall (4,200 sf), insulation (3,500 sf), roofing (2,400 sf), flooring (2,800 sf). Total weight estimate: 85 tons.

steps.label: Find suppliers in your area
steps.exampleOutput: Local lumberyard: in-stock, 5% discount. National: 2-3% discount, free delivery over $5k. Specialty flooring: 1 supplier within 50 miles. Recommend splitting orders for best pricing.

steps.label: Compare your options
steps.exampleOutput: Scenario A (all local): $32k. Scenario B (mixed): $29.8k (7% savings). Scenario C (all national): $28.5k (11% savings, 2-week delay).

steps.label: Watch for long lead times
steps.exampleOutput: Custom flooring: 4-week lead. Specialty doors: 3 weeks. Standard lumber: in-stock. Recommend pre-ordering specialty items within 2 weeks.

workflows.label: Services & utilities
workflows.description: Call 811, arrange dumpster and portable toilet, schedule inspectors, and set up fuel delivery. Check these off so day one isn't a scramble.

workflows.label: Hire your crew
workflows.description: Define roles, post openings, vet candidates with strong refs, and lock in your people before the schedule starts.

steps.label: What role are you filling?
steps.placeholder: e.g., Lead carpenter, framing apprentice, general laborer, MEP coordinator.

steps.label: Flag your top candidates
steps.placeholder: e.g., Mike (framing, 8yr exp, refs solid), Sarah (apprentice, eager, available now).

steps.label: Reach out
steps.placeholder: e.g., "Hey Mike, I've got 12 weeks of framing work starting May 1. Day rate $200, breakfast on us. Interested?"

steps.label: Get an AI outreach draft
steps.exampleOutput: Hey Mike — Chilly here. I've got 12 weeks of framing work on a San Diego ADU starting May 1. Day rate $200, breakfast on us. Your refs are solid — we'd love to have you on. Free for a quick call this week to walk through the build?

workflows.label: Work around the weather
workflows.description: Flag which phases need dry, set pause thresholds, check the forecast, and reschedule around rain so your timeline stays tight.

steps.label: Check the forecast
steps.exampleOutput: This week: 60-70°F, dry. Days 5-6: Rain 60% chance. Days 7-10: Clear. Recommend: Schedule roofing days 2-4, interior work days 5-6, exterior finish days 7-10.

workflows.label: Daily logbook
workflows.description: Record what happened each day via voice or text — progress, issues, safety, visitors, weather. Build your job record as you go.

steps.label: What happened today?
steps.placeholder: e.g., "Framing on west wall done. Electrical rough-in starting tomorrow. Found water stain in south corner, needs investigation." Type or use voice.

steps.label: Categorize today's log
steps.exampleOutput: Solid framing day. Categorized: progress (west wall done), schedule (electrical rough-in tomorrow), quality (water stain in south corner — flagged for follow-up), crew (Mike late by 1hr — note). Tomorrow's priority: investigate water stain before electrical covers it.

workflows.label: Weekly toolbox talk
workflows.description: Run a focused 10-15 min safety talk each week on the job's top hazard. Engage your crew, document attendance, and keep OSHA happy.

steps.label: Specialist drafts the talk
steps.exampleOutput: Topic: Fall Protection. Outline: OSHA 1926.500, harness inspection, anchor point identification, rescue plan. Duration: 10-15 min. Attendees: 8 required.

steps.label: What's the main hazard this week?
steps.placeholder: e.g., Working at height. Excavation adjacent to foundation. Electrical energized panel.

steps.label: Any questions or concerns?
steps.placeholder: e.g., Two asked about harness fit. Mentioned we should review anchor points before Friday.

workflows.label: Expense report
workflows.description: Upload receipts, categorize by trade and expense type, spot budget variance, and explain any overages. Stay on top of spend as the job runs.

steps.label: Specialist codes them
steps.exampleOutput: Uploaded 24 receipts. Categorized: Materials $8,200, Labor $3,400, Equipment $1,200, Other $620. Total: $13,420. Any miscategorizations?

steps.label: Where do we stand?
steps.exampleOutput: Budget: $28,500. Spent to date: $13,420 (47%). On track. Labor overages: +2% (acceptable). Materials: -1% (under budget).

steps.label: Explain any overages
steps.placeholder: e.g., Materials overrun due to spec change for granite. Electrical labor +$400 for unforeseen outlet relocations.

workflows.label: Touch base with vendors
workflows.description: Organize your contact list, request fresh quotes from subs and suppliers, and tally responses to stay ahead of changing rates and availability.

steps.label: Note their rates
steps.placeholder: e.g., Electrician Joe: $85/hr, available 2 weeks out, wants 30-day COI renewal.

steps.label: Write your RFQ
steps.placeholder: e.g., "New 100A subpanel, 12 circuits, labor only, rough + trim, 2-week timeline. What's your all-in?"

steps.label: Tally the responses
steps.exampleOutput: Received 12 quotes for 6 trades. Average response time: 2.1 days. 3 quotes above budget (request revision). 4 quotes excellent (follow up).

workflows.label: Compass check-in
workflows.description: Snapshot your estimate, schedule, and task list. Add a voice note to yourself and test the time-machine feature to track changes over time.

steps.label: Note to yourself
steps.placeholder: e.g., "Client wants to see framing photos before we close walls. Follow up Thursday AM." Type or use voice.

workflows.label: Running punch list
workflows.description: Field-grade running punch list used during construction. Snap a photo, add a one-liner, swipe to resolve. Separate from q24 (the final-walk-through closeout gate).

workflows.label: Manage scope changes
workflows.description: Capture change requests, calculate cost and schedule impact, draft a formal change order, and get signed approval before starting any new work.

steps.label: What's the change?
steps.placeholder: e.g., Client wants to add a third bathroom. Move kitchen island 2 feet west. Upgrade flooring to 4x4 tile. Type or use voice.

steps.label: What's the cost impact?
steps.exampleOutput: Change: Add HVAC zone control system. Materials: $3,200. Labor: $1,400. Contingency (10%): $460. Total CO amount: $5,060.

steps.label: How does it affect the schedule?
steps.exampleOutput: Installation requires 3 additional days during MEP phase. Current timeline: Week 4. No impact on completion date (slack available).

steps.label: Specialist drafts the CO
steps.exampleOutput: CO#001 generated. Format: Project info, detailed change description, cost breakdown, payment terms, approval lines, schedule note. Ready for client signature.

workflows.label: Request payment draws
workflows.description: Calculate % complete, auto-fill your draw form, set holdback, and send to the lender. Get paid as the job progresses, not just at the end.

steps.label: Figure out % complete
steps.exampleOutput: Completed: Demo (100%), Framing (100%), MEP rough (85%). Overall: 62% complete. Eligible for Draw #3: $18,900.

steps.label: Auto-fill the draw form
steps.exampleOutput: Draw #3 form pre-filled: Completion %, eligible amount, project dates, contractor info. Requires review & signature only.
steps.notes: Re-uses the 'draw-calculate' promptId with a different analysis_title and example output.

workflows.label: Collect lien waivers
workflows.description: Track who needs to waive their lien, send conditional waivers with draws, and collect final unconditional waivers before you close the file.

steps.label: Who needs to waive?
steps.placeholder: e.g., Main Electric, Premier Plumbing, Anderson Lumber, Concrete Pros.

steps.label: Build your waiver tracking list
steps.analysisTitle: Mapping who needs what waiver, when
steps.exampleOutput: 4 parties × 2-3 waivers each = ~10 waivers to track. CA uses statutory forms for all. Send today: Anderson Lumber + Concrete Pros (paid in full → unconditional final). Pending draw 3: Main Electric + Premier Plumbing conditional partial.

workflows.label: Payroll check
workflows.description: Review which workers are employees vs. 1099 contractors. Flag anyone who may be misclassified and get CPA sign-off before you finalize payments.

steps.label: Who's on your payroll?
steps.placeholder: e.g., Mike (employee), Joe (1099), Sarah (employee).

steps.label: Run AI analysis
steps.exampleOutput: Payroll classification is intentionally not run by AI. State and federal classification rules turn on facts that vary by worker and state — the wrong answer creates real liability. This step reminds you that classification needs CPA sign-off. Move to the next step (Get CPA advice) with your worker list.
steps.notes: No prompt_id defined in prototype — orphan analysis step.

workflows.label: Final walk-through (substantial completion)
workflows.description: The substantial-completion gate. Walk the job with the client/architect, sign off on the closeout punch list, and clear the path for retainage release. Distinct from the running punch list (q-punch) used during construction.

steps.label: Specialist spots issues
steps.exampleOutput: Analyzed 47 photos. Detected 12 potential punch items: 3 drywall gaps, 2 trim misalignments, 4 paint issues, 3 outlet cover gaps. See detail view.

workflows.label: Collect retainage
workflows.description: Track how much is held back, set reminders to follow up, escalate if needed, and confirm final payment when it arrives.

steps.label: Get a follow-up + lien-deadline plan
steps.analysisTitle: Mapping the cadence + your file-lien deadline
steps.exampleOutput: Retainage of $19,250 due. CA file-lien deadline: 90 days from substantial completion. Cadence: day 7 first written request, day 21 second nudge, day 35 phone call, day 50 certified demand, day 65 pre-lien notice, day 85 file lien if unpaid.

workflows.label: Warranty handoff
workflows.description: Log warranty periods, set reminders to contact clients before expiration, file the paperwork, and close out your project record.

steps.label: Log warranty periods
steps.placeholder: e.g., Roofing 10yr, HVAC 5yr, paint 2yr.

steps.label: Generate the warranty summary
steps.exampleOutput: 6 warranty items mapped. Earliest expiration: GC workmanship + HVAC labor + appliances at 1 year. Register: GAF roof (extends to 50yr), Carrier HVAC (extends labor to 10yr), Andersen windows, Sherwin Williams paint. Owner letter generated for the binder.

workflows.label: What we learned
workflows.description: Capture what went right, what went wrong, cost variance, crew feedback, and key lessons learned. Build wisdom for the next job.

steps.label: What went right?
steps.placeholder: e.g., Client was great to work with. Sequencing was perfect. Team came together well.

steps.label: What went wrong?
steps.placeholder: e.g., Permits took 3 weeks instead of 2. Drywall crew was slow. Material delay on windows.

steps.label: Cost variance
steps.placeholder: e.g., -5 (under budget) or +12 (over)

steps.label: What the crew thinks
steps.placeholder: e.g., "Great experience. Let's do more of these. Safety was tight." Type or use voice.

steps.label: Synthesize your lessons
steps.exampleOutput: Headline: sequencing was a strength, but permit + window-lead-time underestimates ate the budget. Next-job adjustments: add 1 week to permit estimate, order long-lead items at contract signing, pre-qualify drywall subs by job size, keep this framing crew.

workflows.label: Who's asking?
workflows.description: Voice-first lead capture. Dictate the rough shape of a new contact and we'll structure it into the CRM.

steps.label: Capture lead voice
steps.exampleOutput: Captured Mike Tanaka from Tanaka Roofing — re-roof on 2200 sqft tile job in Pasadena. Added to pipeline as a new lead.

workflows.label: Panel schedule (NEC Article 220)
workflows.description: Deterministic electrical service-load calculator. Enter sqft + occupancy and we compute total connected load, apply NEC 220.83 / 220.42 demand factors, and recommend a service size (200A / 400A / etc). Generates a 40-circuit panel directory with auto-balanced legs, breaker sizes, and THHN/THWN wire sizes per NEC 310.16. Exports to PDF. No LLM — all math is reproducible on paper.

steps.label: Optional loads — HVAC kW, water heater, range, EV charger
steps.placeholder: e.g. HVAC 12 kW

steps.label: Calculate + render panel directory
steps.exampleOutput: 4,200 sqft Class B office → 14,700 VA general lighting (3.5 VA/sqft × 4,200). With 12 kW HVAC at 1.25 = 15,000 VA + 4.5 kW water heater = 4,500 VA. Total connected 34,200 VA. Demand applied per NEC 220.42 = ~25,400 VA / 240V = 106 A. Recommended service: 125A single-phase 120/240V.

workflows.label: Equipment schedule (HVAC + plumbing)
workflows.description: HVAC tonnage rule-of-thumb (350 sqft/ton Class B office, 200 restaurant, etc.) plus UPC 422.1 plumbing fixture count (WC / lav / urinal / drinking fountain / mop sink). For commercial TI and new build scoping. Exports to PDF with ASHRAE 90.1, ASHRAE 62.1, UPC 422.1, CPC Table 4-1 citations. Rule-of-thumb only — permit-grade HVAC needs ACCA Manual J/N.

steps.label: Generate equipment schedule
steps.exampleOutput: 4,200 sqft office → 12 tons cooling (350 sqft/ton). Recommend 2× 6-ton RTUs OR 1× 12-ton split. Occupant load 42 (100 sqft/person UPC). Fixtures: 1 WC, 1 lav, 0 urinals, 1 drinking fountain, 1 mop sink per UPC 422.1.

workflows.label: Electrical load calculator
workflows.description: Direct access to the NEC 220 service-load math. Same calculator that powers the panel-schedule workflow — useful when you just need a service-amp answer without the full panel directory.

steps.label: Building type + sqft + optional big loads
steps.placeholder: sqft

steps.label: Returns demand VA + recommended service amps
steps.exampleOutput: See /api/v1/load-calc?buildingType=office&sqft=4200&hvacKW=12 for JSON.

workflows.label: Find an architect of record
workflows.description: Need a CA-licensed architect to stamp your set? Tell us about the project and the Knowledge Gardens team (Charlie, Bou, John, Chilly) personally connects you with one within 1 business day.

steps.label: Tell us about the project
steps.placeholder: Name, email, project type, address, scope, jurisdiction, budget, timeline.

steps.label: We email an architect intro
steps.analysisTitle: Routing to an architect of record
steps.exampleOutput: Request received. Charlie or Bou will email you within 1 business day with an intro to a CA-licensed architect matched to your project's scope, jurisdiction, and timeline.

workflows.label: Submit RFIs
workflows.description: Ask the design team a clarification question — with photos, voice dictation, and a response timer so nothing gets buried.

steps.label: What's unclear?
steps.placeholder: Short subject — e.g. 'Beam pocket detail on north wall'.

steps.label: Describe the question
steps.placeholder: Reference sheet numbers, dimensions, materials. Hit the mic if your hands are full.

steps.label: Attach photos
steps.placeholder: Snap what you're looking at — rear camera, multiple shots OK.

steps.label: Submit to the design team
steps.exampleOutput: RFI filed. The response clock is now running — you'll see it on the list with days-open.

workflows.label: Approvals inbox
workflows.description: Sign off on pending change orders, draw requests, and lien waivers. Every signature captures IP + timestamp + document hash and lands in the audit log. System-of-record for the owner lane.

steps.label: Review what's pending
steps.exampleOutput: 3 items pending: 1 change order ($42,800), 1 draw request ($118,000), 1 lien waiver. Tap any row to review the PDF.

steps.label: Sign or reject
steps.exampleOutput: Signed change order #4. Document hash locked. Counter-party signature pending — both parties must sign for the CO to become a permanent record.

workflows.label: Submit a bid (sub lane)
workflows.description: You're a sub bidding on a GC's project. Build the bid here — scope, line items, CSLB, insurance — and push it to the GC's inbox.

steps.label: Draft scope (AI expand available)
steps.placeholder: e.g. Rough + finish plumbing for ADU. PEX domestic, ABS waste, gas line to range.

steps.label: Add line items
steps.placeholder: Description / Qty / Unit / Unit price — subtotal + tax + total computed.

steps.label: CSLB number + insurance
steps.placeholder: CA license #. Confirm GL / WC / Auto coverage.

steps.label: Submit to the GC
steps.exampleOutput: Your bid is on the GC's desk. They'll review and respond — you'll see the status flip to reviewed, then accepted or rejected.

workflows.label: Sub-bid inbox (GC lane)
workflows.description: You're the GC. Every bid your subs push lands here. Review, accept (drafts a sub-agreement), reject, or counter-offer.

steps.label: Verify CSLB + insurance
steps.placeholder: Click the CSLB link to verify license. Confirm insurance coverage.

workflows.label: Vendor master
workflows.description: Single source of truth for every sub, supplier, and 1099 payee. CSLB#, W-9, insurance, payment terms — the bookkeeper's must-have. Procurement starts here.

steps.label: Add or edit a vendor
steps.placeholder: Legal name, CSLB#, W-9 status, insurance expiries, payment terms.

workflows.label: AR / AP ledger
workflows.description: Money in (billed to clients) and money out (paid to subs). Grouped by project / vendor with payment history. Record payments against any invoice.

workflows.label: QuickBooks export
workflows.description: Month-end close. Export invoices, payments, or vendors as IIF (QuickBooks Desktop) or CSV with GL accounts (QuickBooks Online).

workflows.label: Audit trail
workflows.description: Every insert, update, delete on vendors, invoices, line items, and payments. Filter by table, user, date. Cross-cutting — also available from any stage.

workflows.label: Find a vetted GC
workflows.description: DIY-LANE concierge: tell us about your project and we connect you with 2-3 vetted general contractors in your area within 2 business days. Targets homeowner/dreamer lane.

steps.label: Tell us about your project
steps.placeholder: Name, email, project type, scope, budget, timeline, and anything you're worried about.

steps.label: We send vetted GC intros
steps.exampleOutput: Request received. Charlie, Bou, or John will email you within 2 business days with 2-3 vetted GCs who've done work like yours in your area.

workflows.label: Why does it cost what it does?
workflows.description: DIY-LANE budget walkthrough: each CSI line translated into plain English with a one-sentence explainer of what drives that cost. Targets homeowner/dreamer lane.

steps.label: Walk me through my budget
steps.exampleOutput: Concrete (~$140K): Foundations + slab + footings. Higher in Marin because hillsides need engineered fill and seismic rebar. Framing (~$280K): Wood skeleton — studs, joists, sheathing. About 18% of total — lumber prices have been wild since 2020.
